Two Pointers.description: Uses two index pointers that move towards or away from each other to solve array/string problems without nested loops.
common_traps: Forgetting the sorted pre-condition
Pointer collision — check left < right, not left <= right
Off-by-one on boundary checks
Not handling duplicates correctly
Two Pointers.time_complexity_idea: O(n) — each element visited at most once

Sliding Window.description: Maintains a window over a subarray/substring and slides it forward, avoiding redundant recalculation.
when_to_use: Maximum/minimum sum subarray of size k
Count of subarrays satisfying a condition
common_traps: Not shrinking the window when condition is violated
Forgetting to update the answer before shrinking
Using fixed-size pattern for variable-size problems
Wrong frequency map updates when window shrinks
Sliding Window.time_complexity_idea: O(n) — each element enters and leaves window once
Sliding Window.space_complexity_idea: O(k) where k is window size or alphabet size

Binary Search on Answer.description: Applies binary search on the answer space (not the array index) to find the optimal value satisfying a monotonic condition.
when_to_use: Minimize the maximum / maximize the minimum
Find the smallest k satisfying a condition
common_traps: Choosing wrong lo/hi boundary
Off-by-one in the mid calculation (use lo + (hi-lo)/2)
Not defining the check function correctly
Infinite loop due to wrong boundary updates

Greedy Choice.description: Makes the locally optimal choice at each step, trusting it leads to a globally optimal solution.
common_traps: Applying greedy to 0/1 Knapsack (doesn't work — use DP)
Not sorting intervals by the correct field (end vs start)
Assuming greedy works without a proof of the exchange argument
Missing edge cases with ties
Greedy Choice.time_complexity_idea: Usually O(n log n) due to sorting

DFS / BFS.description: DFS explores depth-first (recursion/stack); BFS explores breadth-first (queue). Both traverse graphs/trees systematically.
when_to_use: BFS: shortest path in unweighted graphs
common_traps: Forgetting to mark nodes visited → infinite loop
Using DFS when BFS guarantees shortest path
Stack overflow with deep DFS (use iterative for large inputs)
Not handling disconnected graphs
DFS / BFS.time_complexity_idea: O(V + E) — visit every vertex and edge once
DFS / BFS.space_complexity_idea: O(V) for the visited set / call stack

Topological Sort.description: Linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge u→v, u comes before v. Only applicable to DAGs.
common_traps: Cycle in graph → no valid topological order
Confusing in-degree with out-degree in Kahn's algorithm
Not initializing all nodes with zero in-degree
Using on undirected graphs
Topological Sort.space_complexity_idea: O(V) for queue and in-degree array

Backtracking.description: Explores all candidate solutions recursively and abandons (backtracks) paths that cannot lead to a valid solution.
common_traps: Forgetting to undo the choice (unmark/remove) before returning
Not adding a proper base case
Modifying state without restoring it (not truly backtracking)
Exponential time — add pruning where possible
Backtracking.time_complexity_idea: Exponential — O(n!) or O(2ⁿ) depending on problem

DP Memoization.description: Top-down Dynamic Programming: solve recursively and cache results to avoid recomputation of overlapping subproblems.
common_traps: Cache key doesn't uniquely identify the subproblem
Forgetting to check cache before recursing
Stack overflow on large inputs — prefer tabulation
Mutable default argument as cache in Python
DP Memoization.space_complexity_idea: O(states) for the cache + O(n) stack

DP Tabulation.description: Bottom-up Dynamic Programming: fill a table iteratively from base cases, eliminating recursion overhead.
when_to_use: When stack overflow is a concern
When all subproblems must be solved anyway
common_traps: Wrong iteration order (must solve smaller problems first)
Off-by-one in table dimensions (size n vs n+1)
Forgetting to initialize base cases correctly
Optimizing space before correctness is proven
DP Tabulation.space_complexity_idea: O(states); can be reduced with rolling array

Heap Pattern.description: Uses a min-heap or max-heap to efficiently access the smallest or largest element in a dynamic dataset.
common_traps: Using a max-heap when a min-heap is needed (negate values in languages without max-heap)
Not handling ties in priority
O(n log n) insertion — consider heapify O(n) for bulk build
Heap vs sorted array tradeoff for static data
Heap Pattern.time_complexity_idea: O(n log k) for top-k problems

Monotonic Stack.description: Maintains a stack in monotonically increasing or decreasing order to answer next-greater/smaller element queries in O(n).
common_traps: Choosing increasing vs decreasing stack incorrectly
Forgetting to pop elements when a smaller/larger one arrives
Not handling the remaining stack elements after full traversal
Index vs value stored in the stack
Monotonic Stack.time_complexity_idea: O(n) — each element pushed and popped at most once

Prefix Sum.description: Precomputes cumulative sums to answer range-sum queries in O(1) after O(n) preprocessing.
common_traps: Off-by-one — prefix[i] usually represents sum of first i elements
Not initializing prefix[0] = 0 as base case
Forgetting to use modular arithmetic for large sums
2D prefix sum formula: careful with inclusion-exclusion
